# Republic: Production Waves - The Real Structure\n\n**Production Waves define WHAT THE PLAYER EXPERIENCES.**\n\nNot infrastructure layers. Not release cycles. **Gameplay progression.**\n\nEach Wave builds on the previous because each Wave's gameplay **depends** on the systems from earlier Waves.\n\n---\n\n## The Dependency Chain\n\n```\nWAVE 0: FOUNDATION\n  Time Engine, Save System, Event Bus, Project Bootstrap\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 1: EXECUTIVE WORKSPACE\n  Campaign HQ, Office, Phone, Email, Visitors, Desk, Calendar, Notifications\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 2: WORLD SIMULATION  \n  Countries, Currencies, Population, Resources, Governments, Constitutions, Culture\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 3: CAMPAIGN\n  Electoral System, Running for Office, Campaign Mechanics, Winning/Losing\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 4: GOVERNMENT\n  Cabinet, Ministers, Executive Powers, Governing, Post-Election\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 5: ECONOMY\n  Production, Trade, Markets, Jobs, Taxation, Revenue\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 6: LEGISLATURE\n  Parliament, Laws, Voting, Factions, Political Parties\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 7: MILITARY\n  Armed Forces, Strategy, Warfare, Defense\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 8: DIPLOMACY\n  International Relations, Treaties, Alliances, Conflict\n  ↓ (needed for)\n\nWAVE 9: MULTIPLAYER\n  Networking, Multiple Players, Political Intrigue Between Players\n```\n\n**Why this order?**\n\nYou can't have Cabinet Meetings without Government.\nYou can't have Government without Campaign.\nYou can't have Campaign without Countries.\nYou can't have Countries without Simulation.\nYou can't have Simulation without Time.\n\n**Each Wave is a prerequisite for the next.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 0: Foundation\n\n**What gets built**: Infrastructure\n\n**What the player sees**: Nothing\n\n**Duration**: 2-3 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Project Bootstrap & Build Pipeline\n- Folder Structure & Organization\n- Logging System\n- Configuration Manager\n- Save System (framework)\n- Event Bus (decoupled communication)\n- Time Engine (60 tick/second)\n- Simulation Core (deterministic stepping)\n- Dependency Injection Container\n- Game Loop (update → render → repeat)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"The skeleton works\"**\n- Project builds without warnings\n- All core systems functional\n- Ready for Wave 1\n\n### Why It Matters\nWithout this foundation, nothing else is possible. Every system from Wave 1-9 depends on these invisible systems working perfectly.\n\n---\n\n## Wave 1: Executive Workspace\n\n**What gets built**: The player's office environment and communication systems\n\n**What the player sees**: Their workspace for the first time\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Campaign HQ (main room, first thing player sees)\n- Office Interaction (move around, interact with objects)\n- Phone System (make/receive calls)\n- Email System (read/write emails)\n- Visitor System (people arrive, you meet them)\n- Desk (where you make decisions)\n- Calendar (schedule view)\n- Notification System (alerts and updates)\n- Transition Manager (moving between locations)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"The player has an office\"**\n- Player can move around office\n- Can make phone calls\n- Can read emails\n- Can receive visitors\n- Can see calendar\n- Immersive workspace environment\n\n### Why It Matters\nBefore we create complex political systems, we need the player to feel like a leader in their office. This is the first impression. The interface for everything that follows.\n\n### Player Experience\n- You sit down at your desk\n- Phone rings\n- You take the call\n- Someone visits\n- You receive an email\n- You check your calendar\n\n**Still no politics. Just the office.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 2: World Simulation\n\n**What gets built**: The world that exists without the player\n\n**What the player sees**: Statistics, reports, world data (but can't interact yet)\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- World Manager (entity lifecycle)\n- Country Entity (nation data model with full attributes)\n- Geography System (terrain, regions, resources, climate)\n- Population System (demographics, growth, migration)\n- Resources System (supply, demand, distribution)\n- Currency System (exchange rates, value)\n- Government Types (monarchy, democracy, dictatorship, etc)\n- Constitution System (rules and laws)\n- Political Culture System (values, traditions, beliefs)\n- Economic Foundation (production chains, basic trade)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"The world lives without the player\"**\n- Simulation runs continuously\n- Countries evolve independently\n- Economic systems functioning\n- Population changes happening\n- Resources flowing\n- No player interaction needed\n\n### Why It Matters\nThe player needs to feel like they're entering an existing, living world. Not a game world that exists to serve them. A world that was already here and will continue without them.\n\n### Player Experience\n- You get reports on your desk\n- \"Your country's GDP is $X\"\n- \"Population grew by 2%\"\n- \"Resource prices are up/down\"\n- \"Trade is flourishing\" or \"Trade is suffering\"\n\n**You can see the world. You still can't change it.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 3: Campaign\n\n**What gets built**: The electoral process - how you gain power\n\n**What the player sees**: The campaign and election system\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Campaign Framework (running for office)\n- Electoral System (voting, campaigns, polling)\n- Political Parties (joining/leaving parties)\n- Faction System (political factions with interests)\n- Campaign Mechanics (rallies, ads, debates, messaging)\n- Public Opinion (approval, favorability, support)\n- Electoral Rules (how elections work in this country)\n- Winning/Losing Conditions (how you win/lose office)\n- Transition System (from civilian to office holder)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"You can run for office\"**\n- Player can enter a campaign\n- Can make campaign decisions\n- Can compete against rivals\n- Can win or lose\n- If you win, you transition to Wave 4 (Government)\n\n### Why It Matters\nBefore you can rule, you must win power. This Wave is about the struggle for legitimacy. Can you convince people to follow you?\n\n### Player Experience\n- You announce your candidacy\n- You campaign across the country\n- You debate opponents\n- You make campaign promises\n- Election day arrives\n- You win (or lose and restart)\n- If you win, you become Head of Government\n\n**Still primarily about YOU gaining power. Not yet about governing.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 4: Government\n\n**What gets built**: Post-election governance - how you rule\n\n**What the player sees**: Cabinet, ministers, executive decisions\n\n**Duration**: 6-8 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Cabinet System (ministers, advisors, specialists)\n- Executive Powers (what you can decide as leader)\n- Government Interface (how you access each function)\n- Advisory System (ministers propose ideas)\n- Decision System (you choose between options)\n- Approval System (public opinion of your rule)\n- Crisis System (crises emerge from simulation)\n- Legitimacy System (power requires mandate)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"You have the power to govern\"**\n- You have won office\n- You have a cabinet of ministers\n- You can make executive decisions\n- Your decisions affect the world\n- Your approval rating changes\n- Ministers compete for resources\n\n### Why It Matters\nNow you have power. But power requires using it wisely. Your cabinet wants different things. The public watches. You must balance competing interests.\n\n### Player Experience\n- Your Finance Minister proposes a tax policy\n- Your Defense Minister wants military spending\n- Your Social Minister wants healthcare\n- You have limited resources\n- You choose what to fund\n- Public opinion responds\n- Approval goes up or down\n\n**Now you're governing. But it's just your office decisions.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 5: Economy\n\n**What gets built**: Economic systems and trade\n\n**What the player sees**: Production, trade, jobs, markets\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Production System (how goods are made)\n- Trade System (importing/exporting)\n- Market System (prices, supply/demand)\n- Labor System (employment, wages, jobs)\n- Taxation System (tax rates, revenue)\n- Business System (companies, industries)\n- Income Distribution (rich/poor divide)\n- Economic Advisors (economists give recommendations)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"Your economy responds to your rule\"**\n- Production changing based on your policies\n- Trade increasing or decreasing\n- Jobs being created or lost\n- Prices reflecting supply/demand\n- Revenue flowing based on tax policy\n- Economic crises possible\n\n### Why It Matters\nEconomics is the beating heart of government. Every decision you make affects jobs, prices, growth, inequality. People feel the economy in their daily lives.\n\n### Player Experience\n- You lower taxes\n- Businesses hire more workers\n- Prices fall\n- People are happier\n- But government revenue drops\n- You have less money for programs\n- You raise taxes\n- Prices spike\n- Unemployment rises\n- People get angry\n\n**Trade-offs. Consequences. Economics is complex.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 6: Legislature\n\n**What gets built**: Parliament, laws, political factions\n\n**What the player sees**: Bills, voting, factions, opposition\n\n**Duration**: 6-8 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Parliament System (legislature with members)\n- Bills System (proposed laws)\n- Voting System (how laws pass/fail)\n- Faction System (political parties in parliament)\n- Opposition System (political rivals)\n- Party Discipline (controlling your party members)\n- Coalition System (building majorities)\n- Constitutional Constraints (what you CAN'T do)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"You must work with parliament\"**\n- You can propose laws\n- Parliament votes on them\n- Other parties oppose you\n- You need majorities to pass legislation\n- Opposition can block your plans\n- Constitutional limits exist\n\n### Why It Matters\nYou're not a dictator. You must convince parliament. Build coalitions. Compromise. This is where democracy becomes real - power isn't absolute.\n\n### Player Experience\n- You propose a law\n- Your party mostly supports it\n- Opposition fights it\n- You negotiate with swing voters\n- The vote is close\n- The law passes by 2 votes\n- You've changed society, but barely\n\n**Democracy is messy, slow, but real.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 7: Military\n\n**What gets built**: Military systems, strategy, warfare\n\n**What the player sees**: Armed forces, strategy, potential conflicts\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Military Framework (armies, navies, air forces)\n- Military Strategy System (where to deploy forces)\n- Warfare System (how conflicts play out)\n- Defense System (protecting borders)\n- Military Spending (budget for armed forces)\n- Geopolitical Tension (when conflicts emerge)\n- Victory/Defeat in Warfare (winning or losing conflicts)\n- War's Impact (refugees, economy, casualties)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"You control military power\"**\n- You have armed forces\n- You can deploy them\n- Wars can break out\n- You can win or lose conflicts\n- Military spending impacts economy\n- Victory impacts legitimacy\n\n### Why It Matters\nMilitary power is the ultimate authority. Wars reshape nations. Military spending competes with social programs. Victories boost approval, defeats destroy it.\n\n### Player Experience\n- Border tensions rising\n- Your general requests authorization\n- You authorize military movement\n- The other side backs down (or doesn't)\n- War breaks out\n- Your side is winning\n- You negotiate a peace\n- Victory gives you legitimacy\n\n**War is the ultimate consequence.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 8: Diplomacy\n\n**What gets built**: International relations, treaties, alliances\n\n**What the player sees**: Other nations, relationships, negotiations\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Diplomatic Relations (how other nations view you)\n- Treaty System (signing alliances, agreements)\n- Trade Agreements (formal commerce treaties)\n- Espionage System (spying on other nations)\n- Alliance System (mutual defense pacts)\n- Diplomacy Interface (negotiating with other leaders)\n- International Incidents (disputes between nations)\n- Soft Power (cultural influence, persuasion)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"You can shape international politics\"**\n- You can form alliances\n- You can negotiate treaties\n- You can isolate enemies\n- You can gain allies\n- International community responds to you\n- Your reputation matters globally\n\n### Why It Matters\nNo nation is an island. International politics shapes everything. Alliances determine wars. Trade relationships determine prosperity. Your reputation is a form of power.\n\n### Player Experience\n- Neighboring nation is threatening war\n- You quickly form alliance with another power\n- The threat backs down\n- You've won diplomatically\n- Later, you need trade partners\n- You negotiate lower tariffs\n- Trade increases, economy booms\n\n**Diplomacy multiplies your power.**\n\n---\n\n## Wave 9: Multiplayer\n\n**What gets built**: Multiple human players, player-to-player politics\n\n**What the player sees**: Other players as nation leaders\n\n**Duration**: 4-6 weeks\n\n### Systems\n- Networking Protocol (deterministic, turn-based)\n- Server Architecture (persistent world)\n- State Synchronization (all players see same world)\n- Player Sessions (join/leave/resume)\n- Turn System (coordinated turns)\n- Emergent Diplomacy (player-to-player negotiation)\n- Player Alliances (humans choosing sides)\n- Player Warfare (humans fighting)\n- Betrayal System (breaking agreements)\n- Emergent Storytelling (players create stories)\n\n### Milestone\n**\"Politics becomes personal\"**\n- Multiple human players in same world\n- Each running their own nation\n- Players form alliances\n- Players negotiate\n- Players betray each other\n- Players cooperate\n- Wars break out between humans\n- Stories emerge from player interactions\n\n### Why It Matters\nThis is where Republic becomes something truly emergent. AI nations are predictable. Human players are not. Betrayal matters more. Alliances mean something. Politics becomes deeply personal.\n\n### Player Experience\n- You're allied with Player 2\n- You share intelligence\n- Suddenly Player 3 attacks you\n- You call Player 2 for help\n- Player 2 joins the war\n- You defeat Player 3\n- Later, Player 2 secretly builds up military\n- You realize they're planning to betray you\n- The political intrigue becomes real\n\n**With humans, the game becomes about reputation and trust.**\n\n---\n\n## Why This Order?\n\n### You can't do Wave 3 (Campaign) without Wave 2 (World Simulation)\n- Campaign needs a world to campaign in\n- Voters need countries to vote from\n- Political opponents need nations to represent\n\n### You can't do Wave 4 (Government) without Wave 3 (Campaign)\n- You need to WIN power first\n- Campaign is how you gain legitimacy\n- Government without campaign = authoritarianism\n\n### You can't do Wave 5 (Economy) without Wave 4 (Government)\n- Economy is how your policies affect the world\n- Government decisions determine economic policy\n- Without government, economy is just simulation\n\n### You can't do Wave 6 (Legislature) without Wave 5 (Economy)\n- Laws are about economic policy\n- Parliament debates tax rates, spending, regulations\n- Legislature WITHOUT economy context = meaningless voting\n\n### You can't do Wave 7 (Military) without Wave 6 (Legislature)\n- Military is controlled by government\n- Military spending is legislative issue\n- Wars are political decisions\n\n### You can't do Wave 8 (Diplomacy) without Wave 7 (Military)\n- Diplomacy backed by military threat\n- Treaties mean nothing without enforcement\n- Military power enables diplomatic power\n\n### You can't do Wave 9 (Multiplayer) without Wave 8 (Diplomacy)\n- Multiplayer is diplomacy between humans\n- Need all other systems working first\n- Human players need a complete world\n\n---\n\n## The Experience Arc\n\n**Wave 0**: You see an office\n\n**Wave 1**: You're in your office\n\n**Wave 2**: You learn about your world\n\n**Wave 3**: You fight to gain power\n\n**Wave 4**: You have power but must use it wisely\n\n**Wave 5**: You see your economic decisions ripple through society\n\n**Wave 6**: You fight parliament to pass your vision\n\n**Wave 7**: You send soldiers to war and people die\n\n**Wave 8**: You negotiate with other leaders\n\n**Wave 9**: You play against humans who can betray you\n\n**The burden of leadership grows with each Wave.**\n\n---\n\n## Timeline\n\n| Wave | Name | Duration | What You Experience |\n|------|------|----------|---------------------|\n| 0 | Foundation | 2-3 wks | Nothing (infrastructure only) |\n| 1 | Executive Workspace | 4-6 wks | Your office |\n| 2 | World Simulation | 4-6 wks | Living world around you |\n| 3 | Campaign | 4-6 wks | Fighting to gain power |\n| 4 | Government | 6-8 wks | Exercising power |\n| 5 | Economy | 4-6 wks | Seeing consequences |\n| 6 | Legislature | 6-8 wks | Compromise and opposition |\n| 7 | Military | 4-6 wks | War and life/death |\n| 8 | Diplomacy | 4-6 wks | International power |\n| 9 | Multiplayer | 4-6 wks | Human intrigue and betrayal |\n| **Total** | **~43-51 weeks** | | **Complete experience** |\n\n---\n\n## One Source of Truth\n\nEach Wave depends on everything before it.\n\nNo skipping.\nNo shortcuts.\nNo partial implementations.\n\nComplete each Wave before starting the next.\n\nEach Wave **works**.\nEach Wave **ships**.\nEach Wave **stands alone**.\n\nBut together, they tell the story of Republic:\n\n**From office worker to world leader. From choosing to ruling. From leading one nation to leading alongside other human players. The burden grows. The responsibility deepens. The stakes raise.**\n\n---\n\n## The Republic Test\n\nBefore adding anything to a Wave, ask:\n\n✅ Does this make the player feel the burden of democratic leadership?\n✅ Does this create meaningful strategic choices?\n✅ Does this affect multiple systems?\n✅ Does this increase replayability?\n✅ Does this enable emergent storytelling?\n✅ Does this reinforce the player's role as Head of Government?\n✅ Does this make the player feel the weight of their decisions?\n\nIf NO to most: **Redesign or omit.**\n\n---\n\n*Republic: From office to empire. From candidate to leader. From simulation to multiplayer. Each Wave builds on the last. No shortcuts.*\n"
